name: Ross Barnett Reservoir
description:
Ross Barnett Reservoir, commonly known as The Rez, is the primary water supply for Jackson and one of Mississippi's most popular fishing destinations. The 33,000-acre reservoir on the Pearl River sits just north of the state capital.

Crappie fishing is legendary, with the annual spring run producing enormous catches of slab-sized fish around brush piles and bridge pilings. Largemouth bass fishing is productive year-round in the flooded timber and grass beds. The lake's proximity to Jackson makes it the most accessible quality fishery for the metro area. Multiple ramps around the lake provide convenient access.

name: Sardis Lake
description:
Sardis Lake is the largest of the four flood-control reservoirs in north Mississippi's hill country, located near the college town of Oxford. The lake's fertile water and abundant structure create an outstanding fishery.

Largemouth bass grow to impressive sizes in the lake's stained water, with fish exceeding 10 pounds documented. Crappie fishing is exceptional during winter and spring, with brush piles producing consistent catches. Catfishing for flathead and channel cats is productive on the lower end near the dam. Multiple Corps of Engineers ramps provide access around the lake.

name: Grenada Lake
description:
Grenada Lake is famous as one of the greatest crappie fisheries in the United States. The Corps of Engineers reservoir in north-central Mississippi regularly produces crappie exceeding 3 pounds, earning it the nickname Crappie Capital of the World.

The standing timber in the lake's many arms provides ideal crappie habitat, with fish stacking up in predictable patterns during winter and spring. Largemouth bass fishing is also excellent, particularly in the upper arms with fresh water inflows. The annual crappie tournament draws competitors from across the Southeast. Multiple ramps handle the significant fishing traffic.

name: Enid Lake
description:
Enid Lake is another of the north Mississippi hill country reservoirs, offering excellent crappie and bass fishing in a scenic setting. The lake is part of the Yocona River watershed and provides flood control for the Yazoo Basin.

Crappie fishing is the primary draw, with the standing timber in the upper lake holding concentrations of fish during the cooler months. Largemouth bass fishing is productive along the lake's creek channels and weed lines. The relatively remote location keeps fishing pressure moderate. Several Corps ramps provide access to different sections of the lake.

name: Mississippi River (MS)
description:
The Mississippi River forms the entire western border of the state, and its associated oxbow lakes, cutoffs, and backwater areas provide some of the finest fishing in the Deep South. The mighty river itself is home to trophy catfish.

Blue catfish exceeding 50 pounds are caught regularly from the main channel, and flathead catfish grow to enormous sizes in the wing dams and riprap. The oxbow lakes along the river — former river channels now cut off — provide outstanding bass and crappie fishing in still water. Multiple ramps and river access points serve different sections of the river.

name: Gulf Coast
description:
Mississippi's Gulf Coast stretches 62 miles along the Mississippi Sound, providing excellent inshore and offshore saltwater fishing. The barrier islands and nearshore reefs create productive habitat for a wide range of species.

Speckled trout and redfish are the primary inshore targets, found around the oyster reefs, grass flats, and marsh edges of the sound. Offshore, the oil platforms and artificial reefs hold red snapper, king mackerel, and cobia. The charter fleet based in Biloxi and Gulfport offers trips to the barrier islands and beyond. Multiple public launches along the coast provide access.
